questions.text: I find that conversations seem layered to me, with a surface meaning and a deeper one running simultaneously.
I find it easy to go along with the group's direction, even when I have reservations.
I find that my capacity for rational, composed analysis during emergencies is the quality that colleagues and friends rely on me for most.
I experience silence after an argument as almost as intense as the argument itself.
I find that my best ideas come when I abandon the plan and follow a sudden impulse.
I believe a premature decision is more dangerous than a delayed one.
I tend to finish one task completely before starting another.
I usually do not notice when someone is hinting at something rather than stating it outright.
I believe it's important to celebrate my own accomplishments.
I can recall specific moments when someone's suffering changed the way I thought about a decision I was making.
I notice that I can be fully engaged in a situation without my emotional volume increasing.
I hold the view that the greatest reward is knowing I made someone else's life better.
I feel that my nervous system processes stimulation at a comfortable, manageable rate.
I find it hard to stay quiet when I disagree with the group's direction.
I prefer to act quickly and course-correct rather than try to predict every consequence.
I think that the ability to suspend emotional reactions during a crisis is a skill that produces better outcomes for everyone.
I tend to make sure my own needs are fully met before turning my attention outward.
I believe that if something is important, it should be stated explicitly.
